Here is what was going wrong, what was changed, and what I could (and could not) verify from here.

## Cause (not “random memory”)

Your **Forge RPROMPT `precmd` hook** did:

```zsh
kill "$_FERGE_RPROMPT_PID"
```

after each command. The async worker is a short‑lived **zsh** job; when it finishes, that **PID is free** and macOS can hand it to the **next** process you start. If you then run **`forge`**, it can get **exactly that PID**. On the next prompt, `precmd` runs again and **`kill`s what is now `forge`** → **`zsh: killed forge`** and exit **137** (SIGKILL). So this was a **PID reuse bug**, not the OS running out of RAM.

The earlier **`pkill -f forge`** was destructive and wrong; I did not run any `pkill` or `kill` against your Forge sessions in this session.

## Fix (applied in `~/.zshrc`)

1. **`_ferge_safe_kill_rprompt_worker`** – only `kill` if `ps -p … -o comm=` is still **`zsh`** / **`-zsh`** / **`*/zsh`**, so a reused PID that is **`forge`** is never killed.
2. **Single background worker** – dropped the inner `{ … } &!` + outer `func &` pattern so **`$!` matches the real rprompt worker**.
3. **`_FORGE_BIN`** – default `~/.local/bin/forge`; plugin init uses it only if executable. Override with **`FORGE_BIN`** if your binary lives elsewhere.
4. **Re-enabled** `add-zsh-hook precmd _ferge_rprompt_precmd` (safe with the guard above).

Relevant section:

```102:147:/Users/kooshapari/.zshrc
# Only reap our rprompt worker (zsh), never a PID reused by forge or another binary.
_ferge_safe_kill_rprompt_worker() {
    local pid=$1
    [[ -z "$pid" ]] && return
    kill -0 "$pid" 2>/dev/null || return
    local comm
    comm=$(ps -p "$pid" -o comm= 2>/dev/null | sed 's/^[[:space:]]*//;s/[[:space:]]*$//') || return
    case "$comm" in
        zsh|-zsh|*/zsh) kill "$pid" 2>/dev/null || true ;;
        *) ;;
    esac
}
# ...
_ferge_rprompt_precmd() {
    _ferge_rprompt_read_cache
    RPROMPT="$_FERGE_RPROMPT_CACHE"
    [[ -x "$_FORGE_BIN" ]] || return
    _ferge_safe_kill_rprompt_worker "$_FERGE_RPROMPT_PID"
    _ferge_rprompt_run_worker &
    _FERGE_RPROMPT_PID=$!
}
add-zsh-hook precmd _ferge_rprompt_precmd
```

## What was wrong

`ulimit -v` is **unlimited**, so that was **not** the cause.

You always see **`[1] + done _ferge_rprompt_run_worker`** right around prompts. That means **every precmd** starts a **background** job that runs **`forge zsh rprompt`**. If you then run **`forge`** (or `forge --version`) in the same shell, you can have **two Forge processes** at once. Many Forge-style apps use a **single-instance** guard and **SIGKILL** the other copy, which matches **`zsh: killed forge`** and **137**.

So this is less “thegent resource management” and more **our zsh integration constantly spawning Forge in the background**.

## What we changed

In **`~/.zshrc`**:

- **Default:** **no** async precmd worker. **One** `forge zsh rprompt` at **shell startup** to set `RPROMPT`, then **no** ongoing background `forge` calls. That should stop the overlap and the **`[1] + done …`** spam.
- **Opt in** to the old behavior:  
  `export FORGE_ENABLE_ZSH_RPROMPT_ASYNC=1`  
  (only if you want live rprompt and accept the clash risk when using the CLI in the same shell.)
- **Opt out** of Forge rprompt entirely:  
  `export FORGE_DISABLE_ZSH_RPROMPT=1`

Here is a **2025–2026–grounded** picture using **primary docs and implementations**, not SEO fluff. Automated **DuckDuckGo HTML** queries from this environment hit an **anti-bot challenge**, so those results were **not** used.

### What is well-sourced today

1. **ZDR (Zero Data Retention)** — **OpenAI’s own platform doc** is the canonical reference: ZDR/MAM are **org-approved** programs; ZDR excludes customer content from abuse-monitoring logs and **forces** `store` to behave as `false` for `/v1/chat/completions` and `/v1/responses` even if clients request otherwise; many endpoints remain **not ZDR-eligible** because they keep application state.  
   - [OpenAI — Data controls / ZDR](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/your-data)  

   **Factored takeaway:** “ZDR on harnesses” is **per-provider** and **contract/dashboard-defined**. OpenAI’s doc does **not** prove MiniMax or CLIProxy behavior; each route needs its own policy reference.

2. **Mesh VPN (your AZ↔LA pattern)** — **Tailscale**’s quickstart is stamped **“Last validated: Jan 5, 2026”** and covers ACLs, SSH, subnet routers, logging — implementation-oriented, not a blog take.  
   - [Tailscale quickstart](https://tailscale.com/kb/1017/install-linux/)  

   **Headscale** remains the standard **self-hosted control plane** implementation (OSS, ~37k stars).  
   - [github.com/juanfont/headscale](https://github.com/juanfont/headscale)

3. **Secrets in git + CI** — **Mozilla SOPS** upstream README (raw) documents **YAML/JSON/ENV**, **age recommended over PGP**, `.sops.yaml` rules, stdin/stdout for pipelines, and **Vault Transit** as a backend — that’s the “real implementation” line.  
   - [getsops/sops README (raw)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/getsops/sops/master/README.rst)  

   **GitHub Actions OIDC** (official GitHub doc) explains **short-lived** federation to clouds and names **Vault** among targets — pairs with HashiCorp’s **JWT/OIDC auth** doc (including **Vault 1.17+ `aud` / `bound_audiences`** behavior, relevant for GitHub’s JWT).  
   - [GitHub — OIDC for Actions](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-for-github-actions/security-hardening-your-deployments/about-security-hardening-with-openid-connect)  
   - [HashiCorp Vault — JWT/OIDC](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/auth/jwt)

4. **Invariant / canonical prose (Vale, “patched” tokens)** — **Vale** is the usual implementation for **controlled vocabulary** in docs; project vocab files + styles are how teams enforce “say `CredentialRef` not informal wording.” Public `vale.sh` docs cover vocabulary topics (one fetch timed out here; use local `vale` docs or the site in a browser).  
   - [vale.sh/docs](https://vale.sh/docs/)  

   **Factored takeaway:** Your `CredentialRef::*` / `ModelRef::*` idea matches **industry patterns**: stable **symbols** + **render/CI patch** (same family as Helm values, OpenAPI codegen, config overlays) — not hand-wavy “AI-LSP” unless you wire an LSP to those symbols explicitly.

Here’s what was wrong and what we fixed.

### Root cause
`agileplus-sqlite`’s `lib.rs` had been **truncated** (to ~150 lines), so `StoragePort`, most of `SqliteStorageAdapter`, etc. were missing. That broke everything that depends on SQLite.

### Fixes applied
1. **`crates/agileplus-sqlite/src/lib.rs`**  
   - Restored from the full **`agileplus-publish`** copy (~1361 lines).  
   - Wired in **`ContentStoragePort`** on `SqliteStorageAdapter` (backlog + feature/WP helpers) so `run_queue` can use `&SqliteStorageAdapter` as `&dyn ContentStoragePort` via generics.  
   - Imports: `backlog` repository module + `backlog::*` domain types + `ContentStoragePort`.

2. **`agileplus-dashboard` seed**  
   - Removed the incomplete **`seed/`** tree (it depended on missing `agileplus_import` and didn’t match `app_state`).  
   - Restored a single **`seed.rs`** from `agileplus-publish` so `seed_dogfood_features()` again returns `(Vec<Feature>, HashMap<...>)`.

3. **`agileplus-triage`**  
   - Dropped unused `chrono`/`serde` imports in `backlog.rs` (from the earlier re-export fix).

### Verified
- `cargo check -p agileplus-cli -p agileplus-subcmds` **passes** (what you need for `task platform:up` / `platform` commands).

### Still failing (optional follow-up)
- `cargo check --workspace` still hits **`agileplus-api`** (missing deps in `Cargo.toml`, `TelemetryAdapter: Clone`, `anyhow` / `Context` in `main.rs`). That’s separate from the CLI/platform path above.

If you want, we can either fix the `agileplus-api` binary next or add `#[derive(Clone)]` + `Cargo.toml` deps so the full workspace goes green.

### Git settings you applied
- **`pull.rebase = false`** with **`pull.ff = only`**: pulls only fast-forward; if you’re behind and can’t FF, Git will stop until you merge or reconcile explicitly.
- **`merge.ff = false`**: merges create a merge commit (not a fast-forward merge), which matches a **merge-based** (not rebase-based) workflow.
- **Aliases** (`sync-upstream`, `sync-origin`, worktree helpers, etc.) are fine as long as they match how you want to integrate with **`origin`** / **`upstream`** on each repo.

## Best long-term path (AgilePlus + this workflow)

### 1. **Dependabot as the only owner of dependency branches**
Stale heads (`ahead_by: 0` vs `main`) are normal when `main` moved on. The durable fix is **not** hand-maintaining dozens of long-lived bump branches.

- Prefer **Dependabot grouped updates** and **versioning policy** so you get fewer, healthier PRs.
- For a stuck branch: **`@dependabot recreate`** or **close the PR and let Dependabot open a new one** from current `main`—that stays aligned with upstream tooling instead of custom merge gymnastics.

### 2. **One PR per line of work; delete branch after merge**
Breadth-first “open PRs for every branch” is useful for **visibility once**, but long term it becomes **inventory debt** (many open PRs, same BLOCKED/UNSTABLE states).

- **Rule:** a branch exists **iff** there is an active intent to land it; otherwise **merge or delete** the branch.
- After merge: **delete head branches** (you already use `--delete-branch` where squash allows).

### 3. **Never parallelize `gh pr create` for the same repo**
Treat that as a **known race**. Long term:

- **Sequential** creation (script or workflow), or  
- A **small GitHub Action** that lists heads without open PRs and creates them **one at a time** with `head`/`base` validated after each step.

### 4. **Align automation with repo merge rules**
You already hit **“merge commits not allowed”** on some repos. Long term:

- **One documented default** per repo: merge vs squash vs rebase, encoded in **branch rules** and **CI**.
- Automation (`gh pr merge`) should **read that policy** (squash where required) so nothing depends on a human remembering.

### 5. **Conflict policy: merge-from-main, not rebase (as you chose)**
Keep **merge `main` into the PR branch** for conflict resolution. Long term, add **CI that runs on the merge result** (or at least `merge` + build) so you don’t fix conflicts and still fail policy gates later.

### 6. **Reduce BLOCKED/UNSTABLE as a separate program**
Opening PRs breadth-first doesn’t fix **reviews + required checks**. Long term:

- **CODEOWNERS** (or small review rules) so BLOCKED isn’t ambiguous.  
- **Required checks** that are reliable and fast.  
- Optionally **stale PR** bot + **auto-close** or **draft** after N days without activity.

### 7. **Where this leaves the three stale Dependabot branches**
**Best long-term:** trigger **Dependabot recreate** (or close and let it reopen) so those branches are **rebased on current `main` by the bot**. Manually merging `main` into old Dependabot branches works but **doesn’t scale** and fights the tool’s model.

---

**Short version:** Treat dependency work as **Dependabot-owned**, keep **one PR per active stream**, **create PRs sequentially**, **match merge/squash to repo rules**, and **recreate stale Dependabot PRs** instead of maintaining a forest of half-dead branches. That’s the path that stays stable as the repo and CI grow.

- After fetching, **`origin/main`** has the squash commit **`c595528b65`**.  
- Your machine may still have the **old two commits** (`dec6c9`, `4a05b`) plus a **merge commit** if you ran `git merge origin/main` — that can leave you **ahead of `origin/main` by a merge commit** and duplicate history.
- **Do not push** that extra merge to `origin/main` if the repo forbids merge commits on `main`.

**Safe alignment options (pick one; none use `reset --hard`):**

1. **Preferred (clean history, no reset):**  
   ```bash
   cd /path/to/thegent
   git fetch origin
   git pull --rebase origin main
   ```  
   If Git complains about duplicate patches, resolve conflicts or use `git rebase --skip` when a commit is empty.

2. **If you must avoid rebase:** create a **new tracking branch** for day-to-day work and leave old `main` as-is until you clean it up manually:  
   ```bash
   git fetch origin
   git checkout -b main-tracking origin/main
   ```

3. **If you already created a merge commit** you want to undo **without** `--hard`:**  
   `git reset --merge HEAD~1` (only undo the merge; I could not run `reset` in this environment).  
   Then `git pull --rebase origin main` as in (1).

Here is a **read-only audit** based on repository paths, `README`s, and manifest snippets under `/Users/kooshapari/CodeProjects/Phenotype/repos`. The tree is large; this focuses on **first-class `phenotype-*` repos** (`README.md` at `phenotype-*/README.md` plus key `template-commons/phenotype-*` mirrors) and **embedded crates/libs** with `phenotype-` package names.

---

## (a) Candidates for neutral / marketable names (generic libification)

These are **domain-agnostic** or **reusable across ecosystems**; the `phenotype-` prefix reads as org-internal rather than product-ready.

| Current | Role (from repo) | Proposed neutral directions (pick one family per stack) |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| **phenotype-infrakit** (+ same crates in **phenotype-shared**) | Rust: event sourcing, cache, policy engine, state machine; README says “generic, domain-agnostic” | **forgekit** / **infrakit** (repo); crates: `forge-event-*`, `forge-cache`, `forge-policy`, `forge-state` (or keep one prefix e.g. `fk-*`) |
| **phenotype-config** (Rust/Python) | Team config + audit trail | **typed-config** / **config-forge** / **vault-cfg** (avoid “phenotype” in PyPI crate name) |
| **phenotype-config-ts** | Zod schema-first TS config (`@phenotype/config-ts`) | **@typedconfig/core** / **@schemaforge/config** |
| **phenotype-auth-ts** | OAuth2/OIDC hex patterns | **@oidc-kit/core** / **@portauth/oauth** (name + scope) |
| **phenotype-go-kit** + **phenotype-go-auth**, **-middleware**, **-config**, **-cli** | Go toolkit split | **portside-go** / **forge-go** with subpackages `auth`, `middleware`, `config`, `cli` (single module) |
| **phenotype-cli-core** | Go CLI utilities | **cmdkit-go** / **forge-cli** |
| **phenotype-go-hexagonal** | Hexagonal layout for Go | Align with existing **go-hex** (`github.com/phenotype-dev/go-hex`) or **port-go** |
| **phenotype-py-hexagonal** (`phenotype-hexagonal` on PyPI) | Python hexagonal helpers | **port-py** / **hex-kit** |
| **phenotype-ts-hexagonal** | TS hexagonal | **port-ts** / **hex-kit-ts** |
| **phenotype-hexagonal** / **phenotype-clean-arch** / **crates/phenotype-xdd** (Rust) | Architecture patterns | **port-rust** / **hex-kit-rs** / **xdd-macros** (split by concern) |
| **phenotype-xdd-lib** | xDD helpers | **xdd-kit** / **spec-kit** (if not tied to org docs) |
| **phenotype-middleware-py** | Python middleware | **port-middleware** / **http-guard-py** |
| **phenotype-logging-zig** / **libs/rust/phenotype-logging** / **template-commons phenotype-logging** | Logging | **structlog-zig** / **tracekit-rs** / **tracekit** (Python) |
| **phenotype-middleware-py** | (see above) | |
| **phenotype-error** (`libs/python`) | Error taxonomy | **typed-errors** / **result-kit** |
| **libs/python/phenotype_sdk** | SDK | **port-sdk** / **forge-sdk** |
| **libs/zig/phenotype-sdk** | Zig SDK | Match chosen Rust/Python name for one brand |
| **template-commons: phenotype-api**, **phenotype-testing**, **phenotype-py-kit**, **phenotype-id** | API scaffolding, tests, kit, IDs | **contract-kit**, **test-forge**, **py-forge**, **id-kit** |
| **phenotype-redis-adapter** (top-level README) | Redis adapter | Fold under **forgekit** adapters or **port-redis** |
| **phenotype-agent-core** (`@phenotype/agent-core`) | Agent core TS lib | **agent-kit** / **runtime-agent** (if meant for reuse outside Phenotype) |

**Note:** **phenotype-design** is mostly **not** a `phenotype-*` npm name—`package.json` is `@kooshapari/design`—but the **folder/repo** is still `phenotype-design`; for a productized story you might rename the repo to **`keycap-design`** or **`design-tokens-keycap`** if you want the repo name to match the package.

---

## (b) Keep-as-phenotype (org- or domain-bound)

Reason: **branding**, **org methodology**, **thin product stubs**, or **documentation hubs**—not primarily reusable libraries.

| Item | Rationale |
|------|-----------|
| **phenotype-design** | Design tokens / VitePress theme “for Phenotype projects”; org-branded surface (aligns with `PHENOTYPE_REBRAND_GOVERNANCE` for owned surfaces). |
| **phenotype-xdd** (large compendium repo) | Org methodology corpus; could be published as a book/site under Phenotype; **not** a crate library. |
| **phenotype-evaluation**, **phenotype-research-engine**, **phenotype-docs-engine**, **phenotype-task-engine** | READMEs are minimal (“Phenotype … framework/engine”); read as **internal product lines** unless you explicitly productize them for external sale. |
| **worktrees/** … **phenotype-docs** | Org documentation drops. |
| **Third-party vendored copy** (e.g. `cliproxyapi-plusplus/third_party/phenotype-go-auth`) | Path stability for that consumer—rename upstream, then bump. |

**Optional middle ground:** **phenotype-agent-core** stays Phenotype-branded if it encodes Phenotype-specific agent protocols; neutralize only if the API is **stable and provider-agnostic**.

---

## (c) Migration risks and sequencing

**Risks**

1. **Multi-ecosystem IDs**: Same concept appears as **GitHub repo**, **Go module**, **npm scope** (`@phenotype/*`), **PyPI**, **Cargo crate names**, and **Zig** package names—one rename touches many lockfiles and CI.
2. **Git dependencies**: e.g. `thegent` references `phenotype-cache-adapter` via `phenotype-infrakit` git URL—**any** crate rename breaks resolution until **every** consumer updates in lockstep.
3. **Duplicate / overlapping trees**: **phenotype-shared** and **phenotype-infrakit** present **the same “infrakit” README** (copy drift risk). Renaming before **deduplicating** doubles work.
4. **Published artifacts**: If anything is on **crates.io** / **npm** / **PyPI**, you need **deprecation windows**, **README redirects**, and **semver** (or new package names).
5. **Docs and URLs**: `documentation = "https://docs.phenotype.dev/..."` on some crates ties the **library** to the org domain—fine for internal docs, but **confusing** if the package is “neutral.”
6. **template-commons** mirrors: duplicate `phenotype-*` under `template-commons/` and worktrees—easy to **miss a straggler** during migration.

**Sequencing (suggested)**

1. **Inventory & consumers**: One graph of “who depends on whom” (grep `phenotype-` in manifests across repos). **Agent mode** can automate this with ripgrep.
2. **Resolve duplication**: Decide **single home** for infrakit-style crates (**phenotype-shared** vs **phenotype-infrakit**) before public renaming.
3. **Pick one namespace strategy**: e.g. **new GitHub org** + consistent prefix (`forge-*`, `port-*`, or org-approved **Helios-*** for owned surfaces per rebrand rules) **before** publishing.
4. **Bottom-up for Rust**: **Leaf crates** (adapters) → **ports** → **aggregating workspace**; **one release train** so `cargo` git deps don’t break mid-stream.
5. **Go modules**: Prefer **one module** with subpackages over many `phenotype-go-*` repos to reduce future rename churn.
6. **npm/PyPI**: **scoped packages** (`@org/lib`) or new names; **major version** bump on rename; **deprecation** notices on old names for one cycle.
7. **Docs + policy**: Update governance docs (`project_decomposition`, `path_governance`) so new work doesn’t reintroduce `phenotype-*` on generic libs.

---

**Summary:** The strongest **neutralization** candidates are **infrakit/shared Rust crates**, **cross-language “kit” repos** (Go/Python/TS/Zig), and **config/auth/middleware** libraries—because they are explicitly generic in READMEs. **Keep Phenotype** on **design**, **xDD compendium**, **thin “Phenotype * engine”** repos, and anything that is **documentation or org workflow** rather than a portable library.

I can switch to **Agent mode** if you want a machine-generated **full dependency graph** (every `phenotype-*` reference) and a **merge plan** for `phenotype-shared` vs `phenotype-infrakit`—that needs a repo-wide search pass beyond this sample.

Here is an audit based on **read-only** inspection of `/Users/kooshapari/CodeProjects/Phenotype/repos` (glob + file reads). The hub is **not** a single git repo (workspace path is a multi-repo tree); findings are about **filesystem layout and hygiene**, not one repo’s `main`.

---

## Prioritized findings

### High

1. **Hub-level names that look like “a normal repo” but are not**  
   Top-level directories named `src/`, `tests/`, and `scripts/` exist and carry the same **AgilePlus + global `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` policy** pattern as other hub entries. That collides mentally and mechanically with the usual meaning of `src/`, `tests/`, `scripts/` *inside* a project and makes the hub look like one broken monorepo.

2. **`worktrees/` layout vs written policy**  
   `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` at the hub describe  
   `repos/worktrees/<project>/<category>/<branch>`, but the tree includes **2-segment** paths such as `worktrees/helios-cli/pr15747/` and `worktrees/AgilePlus/autofix-09b991e/` (project + opaque id), not a stable **category** level. That is inconsistent and hard to navigate or automate.

3. **Parallel “worktree” roots**  
   Besides `worktrees/`, there is a **`.worktrees/`** tree (e.g. paths like `.worktrees/thegent-cli-refactor/...` appeared in glob results). Two conventions for the same concept increase drift and mistaken edits.

4. **`trash-cli/` as a nested junkyard**  
   `trash-cli/` contains a full nested tree including `trash-cli/AgilePlus/bifrost-extensions/...` (thousands of files). That is a **catch-all / recovery clone** sitting next to canonical `AgilePlus/` and `bifrost-extensions/`, which duplicates ecosystem layout and confuses ownership.

5. **`crates/` at hub root with build output in-tree**  
   `crates/` is a real Rust workspace (e.g. `phenotype-hexagonal`, `phenotype-xdd`, `Cargo.toml`) and glob shows **`target/debug/**`** under it. Build artifacts under a long-lived workspace hurt hygiene, clone size, and backup noise unless strictly ignored and not committed.

6. **Many canonical project name variants**  
   Coexistence of **`helios-cli`** vs **`heliosCLI`**, plus `heliosCLI-worktrees`, `worktrees-heliosCLI`, `heliosCLI-broken`, `heliosCLI_resolution`, etc., fights the “one canonical folder per product” rule and complicates scripts and human navigation.

### Medium

7. **ADR vision vs current flat sprawl**  
   `governance/adrs/0005-top-level-directory-structure.md` proposes a **structured** `repos/` with `apps/`, `packages/`, `libs/`, `services/`, `templates/`, `tools/`, etc. The live hub is still largely **flat** with many `phenotype-*`, `template-*`, and app repos side by side—so the **documented target state and the current tree diverge**.

8. **`plans/` at hub root**  
   `plans/` holds a large set of dated strategy/plan markdown files (and duplicates schemas/contracts/scripts from other stubs). That is **hub-level planning documentation** sitting outside a clear `docs/` hierarchy (and overlaps with `governance/` intent).

9. **Duplicate “autofix” worktrees**  
   Multiple siblings like `worktrees/AgilePlus/autofix-*/` and `worktrees/agentops-policy-federation/autofix-*/` suggest **parallel disposable worktrees** without an obvious **retention / archive** story next to canonical repos.

10. **`governance/README.md` links to paths that may not match layout**  
   Quick links reference paths such as `../xdd-methodology-compendium.md` and `../phenotype-architecture-reorganization.md`; those files may live under `plans/` or elsewhere—risk of **broken or ambiguous links** unless the hub root is reorganized or links are made repo-relative to real files.

### Low

11. **`trace/agents.md` vs `AGENTS.md`**  
   Inconsistent agent contract naming (`agents.md` lowercase) vs the rest of the hub.

12. **Typo “fragemented”**  
   Appears in multiple paths (e.g. under `civ/docs/...` per earlier glob, and in `heliosCLI/assets/...`), which hurts search and professionalism.

13. **`helios-cli/perf-results/`**  
   Many timestamped run folders under the canonical repo—worth treating as **generated artifacts** with clear ignore/archive policy (noise at repo top).

---

## 15 concrete rename / restructure recommendations (with example target paths)

1. **Hub policy stubs** — Collapse or relocate misleading top-level `src/`, `tests/`, `scripts/` so they are not mistaken for one app’s layout.  
   *Example:* move contents into  
   `repos/.hub-meta/policy-stubs/{src,tests,scripts}/`  
   or a single  
   `repos/phenotype-hub-config/`  
   with **non-conflicting** names (`stub-src/` not `src/`).

2. **`plans/` hub docs** — Move dated ecosystem plans under a docs namespace.  
   *Example:*  
   `repos/plans/*.md` → `repos/docs/ecosystem/plans/`  
   or  
   `repos/governance/plans/`  
   (align with ADR-005’s `plans/` intent but inside a clear tree).

3. **Worktree depth for PR stacks** — Normalize PR-based worktrees to include a **category** segment.  
   *Example:*  
   `worktrees/helios-cli/pr15747/` →  
   `worktrees/helios-cli/upstream-pr/pr15747/`.

4. **Autofix sandboxes** — Group disposable autofix trees under a parent.  
   *Example:*  
   `worktrees/AgilePlus/autofix-09b991e/` →  
   `worktrees/AgilePlus/_archive/autofix-09b991e/`  
   after merge, or  
   `worktrees/AgilePlus/exp/autofix-09b991e/`  
   while active.

5. **Unify `.worktrees/` and `worktrees/`** — Pick one visible convention.  
   *Example:* migrate  
   `.worktrees/thegent-cli-refactor/` →  
   `worktrees/thegent/cli-refactor/`  
   (or the inverse if hidden is intentional—document it in `governance/`).

6. **`trash-cli/`** — Quarantine or archive so it is not a parallel ecosystem.  
   *Example:*  
   `repos/trash-cli/` →  
   `repos/.archive/trash-cli-<date>/`  
   after extracting anything still needed into canonical repos.

7. **`crates/` + `target/`** — Treat as a real workspace with strict artifact policy.  
   *Example:* rename workspace root to  
   `repos/libs/rust/phenotype-crates-workspace/`  
   and ensure `target/` is **gitignored** (and remove tracked `target/` if present).

8. **Helios CLI naming** — Single canonical directory name.  
   *Example:* keep  
   `repos/helios-cli/`  
   as canonical; move or symlink  
   `repos/heliosCLI/` →  
   `repos/.archive/heliosCLI-legacy-casing/`  
   after migration.

9. **Legacy worktree hub names** — Fold into `worktrees/helios-cli/`.  
   *Example:*  
   `repos/heliosCLI-worktrees/` →  
   `repos/worktrees/helios-cli/legacy-imports/...`

10. **`thegent-broken`** — Align with non-destructive policy: archive prefix.  
   *Example:*  
   `repos/thegent-broken/` →  
   `repos/.archive/thegent-broken-<date>/`

11. **Hub-level `skills/`** — If this is an installable package, name it as such; if not, nest under tooling.  
   *Example:*  
   `repos/skills/` →  
   `repos/packages/phenotype-skills/`  
   (or `repos/tools/agent-skills/` per ADR-005).

12. **Hub-level `sdk/`** — Same as skills: avoid a generic top-level `sdk`.  
   *Example:*  
   `repos/sdk/` →  
   `repos/libs/sdk/phenotype-sdk/`  
   (only if it is truly one deliverable).

13. **`patches/`** — Centralize under tooling.  
   *Example:*  
   `repos/patches/` →  
   `repos/tools/patches/`  
   or  
   `repos/infrastructure/patches/`  
   per ADR-005 buckets.

14. **`contracts/` + `schemas/`** (if they are hub-level shared artifacts) — Align with governance or libs.  
   *Example:*  
   `repos/contracts/` →  
   `repos/governance/contracts/`  
   and  
   `repos/schemas/` →  
   `repos/governance/schemas/`.

15. **`governance/README.md` links** — Point Quick Links at real files after any move.  
   *Example:*  
   `../xdd-methodology-compendium.md` →  
   `../docs/ecosystem/plans/xdd-methodology-compendium.md`  
   (exact path whatever you standardize in step 2).

Here is a distilled checklist grounded in the governance docs under `Phenotype/repos`, with the **canonical org-wide mandate** in `thegent/docs/governance/23_ARCHITECTURAL_GOVERNANCE.md` (dated 2026-03-24, explicitly **mandatory for all Phenotype repositories**).

---

## Source hierarchy

| Scope | Primary doc |
|--------|-------------|
| **Org-wide architecture + xDD** | `thegent/docs/governance/23_ARCHITECTURAL_GOVERNANCE.md` |
| **Ecosystem xDD reference + layout** | `docs/governance/README.md`, `docs/governance/xdd-quick-reference.md`, `docs/governance/architecture-decision-tree.md` |
| **Stricter agent-only testing (thegent-oriented)** | `thegent/docs/governance/TDD_BDD_SDD_GOVERNANCE.md` |
| **Legacy removal / polyglot orchestrator (includes plugin-contract rules)** | `thegent/docs/governance/ARCHITECTURAL_GOVERNANCE.md` |
| **thegent-specific CI harness gates** | `thegent/docs/governance/GOVERNANCE_SUMMARY.md` |

---

## Execution-plan checklist (mandatory constraints)

### Hexagonal (ports & adapters)

- [ ] **Domain-first**: core logic isolated from frameworks, DB, and external APIs.  
- [ ] **Ports**: external needs expressed as interfaces (e.g. repository, bus, filesystem).  
- [ ] **Adapters**: concrete tech lives in adapters (examples named in policy: Bun.SQLite, Vite, `LocalBus`).  
- [ ] **Dependency rule**: dependencies point **inward**; infrastructure and UI depend on domain; **domain depends only on itself**.

### Polyrepo decomposition & libification

- [ ] **Modularize**: pull reusable logic into **shared libraries** (not copy-paste).  
- [ ] **Prefer smaller, focused repos** over monoliths (“micro-repos”).  
- [ ] **Productize** shared libs: **stable APIs, docs, versioning, tests**.  
- [ ] **Design for reuse across repos** (generic enough for multiple consumers, e.g. heliosApp / thegent / AgilePlus).

### Microservices & distributed design

- [ ] **Autonomy**: services **independently deployable and scalable**.  
- [ ] **Communication**: prefer **async messaging** (`LocalBus`) over synchronous RPC when possible.  
- [ ] **Resilience**: **circuit breakers, retries, fallbacks** on service-to-service calls.

### Pluginization / extension & governance orchestration (from complementary policy)

`23` does not use the word “plugin,” but **libification** and **hexagonal adapters** are the main org hooks. Additional **explicit plugin/extension contract** rules appear under the **Polyglot Governance Orchestrator** in `ARCHITECTURAL_GOVERNANCE.md`:

- [ ] **Compatibility contracts**: stable **input/output schema**, **deterministic exit codes**, **versioned plugin API**.  
- [ ] **Adapter first**: wrappers/post-processors before rewriting a checker.  
- [ ] **Policy centralized**: one canonical policy spec; avoid scattered hidden rules.  
- [ ] **Exceptions**: only with governance (owner, reason, expiry).  
- [ ] **Instrument gates**: timing, pass/fail trends, flake rate, cost.  
- [ ] **Runner portability**: POSIX-first core; **language-specific plugins** with **pinned toolchains**.  
- [ ] **Replaceability**: custom extensions have **owner, tests, docs**, and **deprecation/exit plan**.  
- [ ] Pattern hint from `architecture-decision-tree.md`: **Library/SDK → Plugin Architecture + Facade** (when selecting pattern for that class of work).

### xDD (TDD / SDD / BDD) — from `23`

- [ ] **TDD**: **failing unit tests before implementation**; refactor only with **green** tests.  
- [ ] **SDD**: define **data contracts** first (OpenAPI, Zod, Protobuf, etc.); **generate** clients/types from **source-of-truth** schemas.  
- [ ] **BDD**: **Given–When–Then** features; automate acceptance (policy names **Playwright / Cucumber** as examples).

### Engineering standards (`23`)

- [ ] **SOLID**, **KISS**, **DRY** (DRY explicitly tied to **libification**), **CLEAN**.  
- [ ] **Performance**: **P50 / P95 latency** are part of **Definition of Done**.

---

## Stricter xDD / testing overlay (`TDD_BDD_SDD_GOVERNANCE.md`)

Treat this as **mandatory where that document’s scope applies** (it is framed as **agent-only / critical** for thegent-style automation):

- [ ] **Tests before implementation**; **red → green → refactor**.  
- [ ] **No merge without tests**; tests must pass before review.  
- [ ] **Coverage targets stated as 100%** for unit / integration / E2E dimensions in that doc (stricter than generic “80%” norms elsewhere).

---

## CI / policy gate checklist (what the docs actually prescribe)

### Org-wide architectural “gates” (conceptual)

From `23`, CI and review should be able to verify at least:

- [ ] **Layering / dependency direction** (domain not importing infrastructure/UI).  
- [ ] **Contract artifacts** exist and match code for **SDD** (schemas, generated types).  
- [ ] **Acceptance automation** exists for **BDD**-defined behaviors (tooling as per project).  
- [ ] **Service resilience** patterns present for distributed paths (retries/circuit breaking where applicable).  
- [ ] **Latency SLOs** reflected in tests or perf checks as part of DoD.

### thegent-specific mandatory harness (not necessarily every repo)

`GOVERNANCE_SUMMARY.md` defines **merge readiness** for that repo around:

- [ ] **`task quality:sitback-contracts`**  
- [ ] **`task quality:harness-model-contracts`**  
- [ ] Quality workflow is **fail-closed** for those gates.  
- [ ] **Deterministic benchmark smoke** via `task bench:smoke:ci` and locked/offline `cargo bench` invocation as specified there.

Use that block only when the execution plan targets **thegent**; other repos should map to their own CI files while keeping the **`23` architectural rules**.

---

## Tension to be aware of (for plans, not ignored)

`ARCHITECTURAL_GOVERNANCE.md` pushes **zero backwards compatibility** and **no shims** in places, while workspace-level **Phenotype Long-Term Stability** rules often favor **non-destructive** change and `.archive/`. For an execution plan, **call out which policy wins for the program** (product vs internal tooling vs published APIs).

---

| Check | Result | Duration hint |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| `audit` | fail | 2–4s |
| `enforce` | fail | ~2s |
| `gates` | fail | ~4s |

Sample job (`audit`, job id `69047129195`): **`steps: []`**, **`runner_id: 0`**, **`runner_name: ""`**, finished in ~4s. That pattern matches **jobs that never got a runner** (typical for **Actions billing / spending limit**), not “tests ran and failed.”

**Classification:** Treat these workflow failures as **infrastructure / billing-style** (no real execution), **not** as proof the docs scaffold is broken.

### What the ruleset actually requires

Ruleset **“Main Governance Baseline”** (default branch) requires these **status contexts**:

1. **`policy-gate`**
2. **`validate-required-check-names`**
3. **`quality-gate`**

Those names are what block merge from a **ruleset** perspective. On this PR, the rollup lists **`validate-required-check-names`** (failed fast) but **does not show** `policy-gate` or `quality-gate` at all — so you may also be **missing** expected checks (or they’re published under different names than the ruleset expects).

Classic **branch protection** API for `main` returns **404** (“Branch not protected”); governance is via **rulesets** only.

### `gh pr merge --admin --squash`

- Ruleset **allows** squash (among merge/rebase/squash).
- Ruleset detail: **`bypass_actors: []`**, and for the authenticated user the API reports **`current_user_can_bypass`: `never`** — so **you cannot assume** `--admin` will override required checks; it **may still be blocked** if administrators are not allowed to bypass this ruleset.
- **Do not merge** without your own explicit decision: confirm in **Repo → Rules → Rulesets** whether admins can bypass, or try the merge and accept that it might fail with a ruleset error.

**Bottom line:** Merge is **BLOCKED** mainly by **required status checks** (`validate-required-check-names` failed in seconds; **`policy-gate` / `quality-gate`** absent from the rollup vs ruleset). Failures align with **billing/no-runner**, but GitHub still treats them as failing/missing required checks until that’s fixed or bypassed. **`gh pr merge --admin --squash` is uncertain** without confirming ruleset bypass for admins; **no merge was performed.**

---

### Three recommended first “contract extraction” targets (Wave A)

1. **`pheno.ports.mcp`**  
   **Rationale:** Already a clean protocol bundle with a **single adapter package** and tests; ideal to freeze as versioned contracts (e.g. OpenAPI for HTTP surfaces that sit behind MCP, or JSON Schema for tool/resource payloads) because changes ripple through schemes, manager, and adapters.

2. **`pheno.ports.auth.providers` (`AuthProvider`, `MFAAdapter`, and related types in `providers.py`)**  
   **Rationale:** **Only** port family with direct imports from `src/pheno/adapters/` today; security-sensitive and stable as an external integration boundary (OAuth/MFA). Good candidate for explicit machine-readable contract (e.g. JSON Schema for token/credential shapes) alongside the protocols.

3. **`pheno.ports.stream` (message / envelope types)**  
   **Rationale:** Shared DTOs used by **`stream.py` and examples**; smaller surface than MCP but defines cross-runtime streaming semantics. Extracting schema here reduces drift between gateway examples and core types before more adapters appear.

## PR 44 — `KooshaPari/colab` (“docs: complete docs-site scaffold”)

### Why merge is blocked

1. **Review gate** — `reviewDecision` is **`CHANGES_REQUESTED`**. GitHub will not satisfy “approved review” rules until that is cleared.
2. **`mergeStateStatus`: `BLOCKED`** — Consistent with the above plus failing checks (exact branch-protection rules are on the repo, but the rollup shows real failures).
3. **Checks** — Several runs are **`FAILURE`**: **`typecheck`**, multiple **`guard`** jobs (Security Guard / Hooks), and **`ci-summary`**. **`lint`**, **`unit-tests`**, and **`coverage`** are **`SKIPPED`** (typical when earlier CI stages fail). **`secret-scan`** and **`CodeRabbit`** (status context) report success; the **PR review** from CodeRabbit is still “changes requested.”

So: **blocked by (a) a changes-requested review and (b) red CI**, not by merge conflicts (`mergeable` is **`MERGEABLE`**).

### Who requested changes

**[CodeRabbit](https://github.com/apps/coderabbitai)** — review author **`coderabbitai`** / **`coderabbitai[bot]`**, submitted **2026-03-29**, state **`CHANGES_REQUESTED`**, with **14 actionable inline comments** (grammar, lockfile, VitePress config/locales, `site-meta` nav link, stray backslashes in locale `index.md` files, root vs `docs` `package.json` wiring, VitePress CLI args (`docs` vs `.`), Deno → Node `test`, Playwright `webServer`, empty **E5: Terminal UI** section in `PRD.md`, etc.).

No human reviewer appears in the `reviews` payload you asked for—only that bot review.

### Dismiss review vs push fixes

- **Dismissing** the CodeRabbit review might unblock *review* requirements **if** your rules allow dismissal and nothing else requires CodeRabbit green—but it **does not fix** failing **`typecheck`** / **`guard`** / **`ci-summary`**. Merging with those still red would violate normal “fix CI on the PR” expectations unless you explicitly use admin override and accept the risk.
- **Pushing fixes** is the **right default path**: align the branch with what CI enforces, then either satisfy or thoughtfully respond to CodeRabbit (many items match real breakage risk, e.g. wrong VitePress root, `Deno.test` under Node, missing Playwright `webServer`).

**Do not treat “dismiss bot” as a substitute for fixing red jobs** unless maintainers have explicitly decided bot + CI policy work that way.

### Actionable next steps

1. **Open failing jobs** and fix from logs (examples from rollup):  
   - [CI typecheck job](https://github.com/KooshaPari/colab/actions/runs/23701880649/job/69047157665) (and the other `typecheck` run if still relevant).  
   - [Security Guard (Hooks) guard job](https://github.com/KooshaPari/colab/actions/runs/23701880653/job/69047157597) (and sibling `guard` failures).  
   Until **`typecheck`** and **`guard`** pass, **`ci-summary`** will likely stay red and downstream jobs may stay skipped.

2. **Implement high-signal doc/tooling fixes** that match both CI and CodeRabbit (verify locally before push):  
   - `docs/package.json`: VitePress scripts should use **`.`** as site root, not **`docs`**.  
   - Replace **`Deno.test`** with **`node:test`** in the listed test files.  
   - Add **`webServer`** to Playwright config so e2e can start the dev server.  
   - **`docs/.gitignore`**: stop ignoring **`package-lock.json`** if you want a tracked lockfile; regenerate and commit.  
   - Quick content fixes: stray **`\`** in fa/zh pages; **`site-meta`** Home link; optional **`ADR.md`** hyphenation; **`PRD.md`** E5 section.

3. **Wire root `package.json`** so CI/root scripts run **`docs:build` / `docs:test` / `docs:check`** (or workspace equivalent), if that’s what your repo’s CI expects.

4. **Push one or more commits** to the PR branch; let CodeRabbit re-run. If after fixes the bot is still **`CHANGES_REQUESTED`** on nit-only items, **a maintainer** can decide to dismiss or reply—**after** CI is green and a human agrees the remaining points are non-blocking.

5. **Merge only when** required reviews and required checks match your policy (per your instruction: **no merge** from this pass; this is **not** trivially safe without addressing CI + review state).

---

**Summary line:** Merge is blocked by **CodeRabbit’s `CHANGES_REQUESTED` review** and **failing CI (`typecheck`, `guard`, `ci-summary`)**. **Push fixes** (especially CI + the structural doc/test issues CodeRabbit listed); **dismissing** the bot without fixing CI is the wrong trade unless you explicitly accept broken checks and use admin merge.

### `gh pr view 44 --json mergeStateStatus,mergeable`


## Cause

CodeQL was using **`languages: javascript`** with **`npm ci && npm run build`** between `init` and `analyze`. For this VitePress layout, JS/TS/Vue live under **`.vitepress/`** and **`docs/.vitepress/`**, not the repo root. That setup matches the usual failure mode: JS/TS is detected, but the run ends with **no usable source during the “build”** / nothing processed.

## Fix (stable, still CodeQL)

1. **`javascript-typescript`** with **`build-mode: none`** — analyze interpreted source without relying on a traced compiler build (appropriate for VitePress).
2. **`.github/codeql/codeql-config.yml`** — explicit **`paths`** for common extensions (including **`.vue`** and **`.mts`**) and **`paths-ignore`** for `node_modules` / `dist`.
3. **Order**: checkout → **Node + `npm ci`** → **init** → **analyze** (no `vitepress build` in this job).
4. **PR `paths`**: include **`.vue`**, **`.mts`**, **`.mjs`/`.cjs`**, and workflow/config paths so config-only edits still run CodeQL.

## Commit

**SHA:** `ac70879b669fed7bc84fba8deab9c44aef421ab3`

- **`git push upstream feat/full-turn-linear-20260326`** → **rejected** by GitHub rules: *“Changes must be made through a pull request”* (rules on that branch).
- **`git push origin feat/full-turn-linear-20260326`** → **succeeded** (your **Airlock** remote: `/Users/kooshapari/.airlock/repos/1112c420a861.git`).

So the fix is on **origin**; **`upstream` (github.com/KooshaPari/phenodocs) does not have this commit yet** until you promote/sync Airlock → GitHub (or use whatever path your rules allow).

| **open-prose** | [https://github.com/andrei-shtanakov/open-prose](https://github.com/andrei-shtanakov/open-prose) | **OpenProse**: a “language for AI sessions” — markdown/`.prose` plus an LLM-backed **VM** to orchestrate **multi-agent** workflows (no traditional binary runtime). | *(GitHub shows no dominant language; repo is Markdown + `.prose` + specs)* | [README](https://github.com/andrei-shtanakov/open-prose/blob/main/README.md) · VM/spec entrypoint: [prose.md](https://github.com/andrei-shtanakov/open-prose/blob/main/prose.md) |

## Synthesis (AI / agent engineering)

These three sit at different layers of the same problem: **how we specify, measure, and run agentic work**. **gocognit** is classic **software quality**: it quantifies human-facing complexity in **Go** code, which matters when agents churn out lots of control flow and you want CI or reviewers to enforce a complexity budget. **stef-k/spec** is **spec → plan → tasks**: it turns informal design prose into a **structured, agent-executable** task list with minimal ceremony. **open-prose** pushes further into **orchestration**: it treats a **documented VM** (`prose.md`) plus `.prose` programs as the contract, with the **LLM acting as the VM** and features like `session` mapping to real **subagents**. Together, they sketch a pipeline from **maintainable code** (gocognit) → **grounded work breakdown from specs** (spec) → **explicit multi-agent control flow** (open-prose).

### 1. `colab: resolve_duplicate_origin_with_heliosApp-colab` (G006)

| Evidence | Detail |
|----------|--------|
| **Same `origin` URL** | Both folders use **`https://github.com/KooshaPari/colab.git`** for `origin` (fetch and push). |
| **Nature of “duplicate”** | This is **two local checkouts of one GitHub repo**, not two different `origin` hosts. There is no split between e.g. `KooshaPari/colab` vs another org on `origin`. |
| **Blackboard remote** | **colab**: remote name **`blackboard`** → `https://github.com/blackboardsh/colab.git`. **heliosApp-colab**: remote name **`upstream`** → same `blackboardsh/colab` URL. Naming differs; URL is the same upstream product repo. |
| **Divergence** | **`colab`** `main`: `## main...origin/main [ahead 20]` (dirty: modified + untracked). **`heliosApp-colab`** `main`: `## main...origin/main [ahead 16, behind 8]` (dirty). Same remote branch, different local history vs each other. |

**G006 implication:** “Duplicate origin” here is best read as **duplicate working trees / clones**, not conflicting `origin` URLs. Resolution is **workspace policy** (which directory is canonical for daily work, how to avoid double-push/double-merge confusion), not retargeting `origin` on one checkout.

### 2. `colab: decide_archive_or_rename_posture_for_heliosApp-colab`

| Factor | Evidence |
|--------|----------|
| **Not a separate GitHub product** | `heliosApp-colab`’s `origin` is still **KooshaPari/colab**; it is not a differently named fork as `origin`. |
| **Upstream labeling** | **heliosApp-colab** uses conventional **`upstream`** → `blackboardsh/colab`. **colab** uses **`blackboard`** for the same URL — less standard for fork/upstream docs. |
| **Local-only differentiation** | Folder name `heliosApp-colab` suggests a **Helios-branded or app-specific lane**; that is local naming unless mirrored by a separate remote (it is not, on `origin`). |
| **Docs in tree** | **heliosApp-colab** contains `docs/branch-health-reconcile-2026-03-03.md` describing reconciliation with **`upstream/main`** and merge discipline — evidence this checkout was used for **upstream sync / branch health** work. |

**Posture (evidence-based, no git writes):**

- **Do not archive** as “obsolete repo” in the GitHub sense — there is only one **`origin`** repo; archiving would be a **folder** decision in Phenotype layout, not a GitHub archive of a second product.
- **Rename** is optional and **cosmetic** (directory name); it does not change remotes. Any rename should match team language (e.g. `colab-helios-worktree` vs `heliosApp-colab`) and docs that reference the path.
- **Keep as derivative / second lane** is supported by: distinct ahead/behind on `main`, extra branch docs, and typical use of a second clone for parallel integration (OXC migration branch refs appeared in `git branch -vv` for **heliosApp-colab**).

### 4. Recommended posture (summary)

| Topic | Recommendation |
|-------|------------------|
| **Canonical checkout name** | Treat **one** directory under `repos/` as the **canonical Phenotype working copy** for `KooshaPari/colab` (policy choice: either **`colab`** or **`heliosApp-colab`**, not both as “primary” without a written rule). Evidence: **`heliosApp-colab`** has clearer **`upstream`** naming and branch-reconcile notes; **`colab`** is the shorter name and also tracks `main` with a large local ahead count. |
| **heliosApp-colab** | **Keep** as a **second clone / integration lane** (or **rename** the folder for clarity), **not** as a separate GitHub `origin`. **Archive** only if the team explicitly retires that path and merges all unique work — there is no separate remote to “archive” here. |
| **Align remotes** | Standardize remote names across clones (**`origin`** = `KooshaPari/colab`, **`upstream`** = `blackboardsh/colab`) so G006/G007 docs match `git remote -v` everywhere. |

### 5. Draft upstream policy bullets (KooshaPari/colab vs blackboardsh / blackboard remote)

1. **`origin` is always `https://github.com/KooshaPari/colab.git`** for Phenotype-owned development, PRs, and CI that apply to this fork.  
2. **`https://github.com/blackboardsh/colab.git` is upstream (vendor)** — fetch/merge policy for absorbing upstream changes; use a single remote name (e.g. **`upstream`**) in docs and scripts.  
3. **Do not point `origin` at `blackboardsh/colab`** in Phenotype clones intended for Koosha-owned delivery; keep vendor and fork roles separate.  
4. **Multiple local folders (`colab` vs `heliosApp-colab`) are not multiple origins** — they are duplicate checkouts of the same `origin`; only one should be designated canonical for merge/push to avoid drift.  
5. **When publishing or cloning for end users**, upstream README may reference `blackboardsh/colab`; Phenotype governance docs should state that **day-to-day contribution** targets **`KooshaPari/colab`** and **`upstream`** is for syncing from Blackboard.

# AGENTS Guide

---

## Why these matter for dev workflows

- **Mautic** — Relevant when your workflow includes **owned marketing data**, **custom integrations**, or **avoiding SaaS lock-in**: you run and extend a full PHP application (plugins, APIs) instead of only consuming a hosted martech API.
- **gci** — Relevant for **Go code hygiene and CI**: stable **import grouping and ordering** cuts noisy diffs, matches team/style rules, and aligns with tools like **golangci-lint** that can enforce `gci` — less churn on reviews and merges.
- **SSVC** — Relevant when **security and release** work must be **explainable**: patch order and communication align with **stakeholder-specific** risk (e.g., safety vs. mission vs. public exposure), improving triage memos, SLAs, and cross-team agreement beyond a single severity score.

Sources: [GitHub API](https://api.github.com/repos/mautic/mautic), [daixiang0/gci](https://api.github.com/repos/daixiang0/gci), [CERTCC/SSVC](https://api.github.com/repos/CERTCC/SSVC); README snippets from [mautic/mautic `7.x` README](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mautic/mautic/7.x/README.md) and [gci README](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/daixiang0/gci/master/README.md).

## Recommendation: should `agent-devops-setups` normalize the **default branch to `main`**?

**Yes, as the steady-state target** — with one explicit caveat about **in-flight work**.

**Evidence**

1. **GitHub default is not `main`:** `origin` HEAD branch is **`agentops/policy-federation-rollout`**, while the repo still maintains a **`main`** branch.
2. **Local policy docs expect `main`:** `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md` in this repo tell agents to keep the **primary checkout on `main`** and integrate via merge/cherry-pick — that matches org worktree rules, not a long-lived non-`main` default.
3. **Automation is `main`-biased:** `validate-policy.yml` runs **`push` workflows only for `main`**. Making a non-`main` branch the **default** splits “where people push” from “where validation runs on push,” unless you duplicate branch filters or merge everything through `main`.
4. **Divergence:** `agentops/policy-federation-rollout` is **3 commits ahead** of `main`; normalization should mean **merging that work into `main` first**, then switching GitHub default to `main`, then deleting or archiving the rollout branch policy as appropriate — not flipping default with unreconciled history.

**When *not* to rush:** If the **intention** is a **temporary** campaign branch as default for a controlled rollout, document that explicitly and align `validate-policy.yml` `push` branches (or rely only on PR gates) until the campaign completes.

### 11. `agentops-policy-federation` — `.github/workflows/required-check-names-guard.yml` (first 80 lines + remainder)

**What it validates**

- On **pull_request**, job **`validate-required-check-names`** runs embedded **Python** that:
  - Parses **`required_checks`** from every `policies/**/*.yml` (inline list or `- item` bullets).
  - Collects “available” check names from **`.github/workflows/*.yml`**: each **`jobs.<job_id>`** key and each job’s **`name:`** (when present).
  - Computes **`missing = configured − available`**; prints configured list, discovered names, and missing; **`sys.exit(1)`** if any required name is not found on a workflow job id/name.

**Actionable:** Any policy change that adds a required check must have a matching workflow job **id** or **`name:`** string, or the guard fails.

```markdown
# cliproxyapi-plusplus

English | [Chinese](README_CN.md)

This is the Plus version of [cliproxyapi-plusplus](https://github.com/kooshapari/cliproxyapi-plusplus), adding support for third-party providers on top of the mainline project.

All third-party provider support is maintained by community contributors; cliproxyapi-plusplus does not provide technical support. Please contact the corresponding community maintainer if you need assistance.

The Plus release stays in lockstep with the mainline features.

## Differences from the Mainline

- Added GitHub Copilot support (OAuth login), provided by [em4go](https://github.com/em4go/CLIProxyAPI/tree/feature/github-copilot-auth)
- Added Kiro (AWS CodeWhisperer) support (OAuth login), provided by [fuko2935](https://github.com/fuko2935/CLIProxyAPI/tree/feature/kiro-integration), [Ravens2121](https://github.com/Ravens2121/CLIProxyAPIPlus/)

## New Features (Plus Enhanced)

- **OAuth Web Authentication**: Browser-based OAuth login for Kiro with beautiful web UI
- **Rate Limiter**: Built-in request rate limiting to prevent API abuse
- **Background Token Refresh**: Automatic token refresh 10 minutes before expiration
- **Metrics & Monitoring**: Request metrics collection for monitoring and debugging
- **Device Fingerprint**: Device fingerprint generation for enhanced security
- **Cooldown Management**: Smart cooldown mechanism for API rate limits
- **Usage Checker**: Real-time usage monitoring and quota management
- **Model Converter**: Unified model name conversion across providers
- **UTF-8 Stream Processing**: Improved streaming response handling

## Kiro Authentication

### Web-based OAuth Login

Access the Kiro OAuth web interface at:

```
http://your-server:8080/v0/oauth/kiro
```

This provides a browser-based OAuth flow for Kiro (AWS CodeWhisperer) authentication with:
- AWS Builder ID login
- AWS Identity Center (IDC) login
- Token import from Kiro IDE
```

```1:76:/Users/kooshapari/CodeProjects/Phenotype/repos/packages/thegent-core/src/thegent_core/models/__init__.py
"""Model catalog and distributed routing."""

from __future__ import annotations

import importlib
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any

from thegent_core.models.catalog import (
    CatalogView,
    ModelCatalog,
    ResolvedRoute,
    Route,
    filter_models_for_provider,
    normalize_model_id,
    normalize_route_policy,
    resolve_route,
    resolve_route_contract,
    route_contract,
)
from thegent_core.models.quality_values import invalidate_quality_index_cache
from thegent_core.models.speed_values import invalidate_speed_index_cache
from thegent_core.models.task_io import TaskError, TaskInput, TaskOutput, TaskSpec

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from thegent_core.models.scrapers import (
        SCRAPER_REGISTRY,
        ModelScraper,
        get_scraped_catalog,
        invalidate_models_cache,
        scrape_all,
    )

_SCRAPER_LAZY_NAMES = frozenset(
    {
        "SCRAPER_REGISTRY",
        "ModelScraper",
        "get_scraped_catalog",
        "invalidate_models_cache",
        "scrape_all",
    }
)

def __getattr__(name: str) -> Any:
    if name in _SCRAPER_LAZY_NAMES:
        mod = importlib.import_module("thegent_core.models.scrapers")
        value = getattr(mod, name)
        globals()[name] = value
        return value
    msg = f"module {__name__!r} has no attribute {name!r}"
    raise AttributeError(msg)

__all__ = [
    "SCRAPER_REGISTRY",
    "CatalogView",
    "ModelCatalog",
    "ModelScraper",
    "ResolvedRoute",
    "Route",
    "TaskError",
    "TaskInput",
    "TaskOutput",
    "TaskSpec",
    "filter_models_for_provider",
    "get_scraped_catalog",
    "invalidate_models_cache",
    "invalidate_quality_index_cache",
    "invalidate_speed_index_cache",
    "normalize_model_id",
    "normalize_route_policy",
    "resolve_route",
    "resolve_route_contract",
    "route_contract",
    "scrape_all",
]
```

I've completed my research on the `policy-federation` hooks and the "guardian" logic across the Phenotype repositories. Below is a detailed summary of my findings.

### 1. Hook Definitions and Locations
The hooks `policy-federation:ask` and `policy-federation:devops-ask-networked-installs` are managed primarily by the `agentops-policy-federation` repository.

*   **Hook Generator**: The logic for generating these hook IDs is located in `agentops-policy-federation/cli/src/policy_federation/claude_hooks.py`. It evaluates the decision from a central policy engine and returns a `permissionDecisionReason` prefixed with `policy-federation:`.
*   **Specific Hook Rule**: The hook `policy-federation:devops-ask-networked-installs` refers to a specific policy rule defined in `agentops-policy-federation/policies/task-domain/devops-shared.yaml`:
    ```yaml:9:20:agentops-policy-federation/policies/task-domain/devops-shared.yaml
          - id: "devops-ask-networked-installs"
            description: "Networked package installation requires an explicit approval path."
            priority: 50
            effect: ask
            actions: ["exec"]
            match:
              command_patterns:
                - "uv pip install*"
                - "pip install*"
                - "npm install*"
                - "pnpm add*"
                - "brew install*"
    ```

### 2. Notification Mechanism
The hooks communicate with Claude Code via the `PreToolUse` event. 

*   **Output Suppression**: In `claude_hooks.py`, any decision that results in an `allow` (either directly from policy or after a "guardian" review) explicitly suppresses output:
    ```python:249:250:agentops-policy-federation/cli/src/policy_federation/claude_hooks.py
        if result["final_decision"] == "allow":
            return {"continue": True, "suppressOutput": True}
    ```
*   **Permission Decision**: If the decision is `deny` or `ask`, the hook returns the rule ID as the `permissionDecisionReason`, which Claude Code then uses in its own tool-gating UI.

### 3. The "Guardian" Feature (Headless Review)
The "guardian" feature is an automated, agent-first bypass mechanism implemented as a "Headless Reviewer."

*   **Logic Location**: `agentops-policy-federation/cli/src/policy_federation/headless_review.py`.
*   **Mechanism**: When a policy rule returns an `ask` effect, the system (in `interceptor.py`) checks if `ask_mode` is set to `review`. If so, it calls Codex (the 3rd party "guardian" agent) to arbitrate the decision.
*   **Bypass Behavior**: If the guardian (Codex) can confidently decide to `allow` or `deny` the command, the system uses that decision. If the guardian allows it, the command proceeds silently without user intervention.

### 4. Why Notifications Are Missing
Based on the code, the user is likely not seeing notifications because:
1.  **Silent Approvals**: The system is designed to be silent for `allow` decisions. If a command is allowed by the base policy, the hook returns `suppressOutput: True`.
2.  **Guardian Resolution**: If the "guardian" (headless reviewer) is active and successfully resolves an `ask` decision to `allow`, it is also treated as a silent approval.
3.  **Default Mode**: The default `POLICY_ASK_MODE` is `review` (`agentops-policy-federation/cli/src/policy_federation/constants.py`), which prioritizes the headless agent over the user.

### 5. 3rd Party Libraries and Tools
*   **Codex**: The primary 3rd party "guardian" tool. It is invoked via the binary specified in `POLICY_REVIEW_BIN` (defaulting to `codex`) using the `gpt-5.4-mini` model.
*   **Policy Federation Tools**: Located in `agent-devops-setups/tools/`, these scripts manage the distribution of policies across the 50 repositories in the Phenotype org:
    *   `federate_policy.py`: Resolves layered policies into an effective payload.
    *   `onboard_repos.sh`: Automates the setup of the "Security Guard" across multiple repositories.
*   **Policy Contract**: The `policy-contract` repository provides shared primitives and evaluators for the policy language used by the federation logic.

### Research Summary Table
| Feature | Implementation Location |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hook IDs** | `agentops-policy-federation/cli/src/policy_federation/claude_hooks.py` |
| **Rule Definitions** | `agentops-policy-federation/policies/task-domain/devops-shared.yaml` |
| **Guardian Logic** | `agentops-policy-federation/cli/src/policy_federation/headless_review.py` |
| **Federation Tools** | `agent-devops-setups/tools/federate_policy.py` |
| **Interception** | `agentops-policy-federation/cli/src/policy_federation/interceptor.py
` |

**Analyzing System Components**

My research phase is complete. I now have a solid understanding of hook usage, notification mechanics, the guardian feature, and potential reasons for missed notifications. I'm preparing the final response now.

The review of 3rd party libraries, research papers, and policy-guardian systems within the Phenotype repositories is complete. The analysis focused on the "friction-risk balance" as requested.

### 1. 3rd Party Libraries and Tools Identified
The Phenotype ecosystem leverages a tiered stack of 3rd party tools, primarily managed via `uv` (Python), `bun` (TypeScript/JS), and `cargo` (Rust).

*   **LLM & AI Orchestration**:
    *   `litellm`: Central gateway supporting 50+ providers; used to unify model access (e.g., `gpt-5.4-mini`).
    *   `fastmcp`: Core framework for the Model Context Protocol (MCP) server implementation.
    *   `playwright`: Used for high-fidelity browser-based research and tool execution.
*   **Policy & Governance**:
    *   **Open Policy Agent (OPA) / Rego**: Industry-standard policy-as-code engine for decoupled decision-making.
    *   **Oso (Polar)**: Relationship-based access control (ReBAC) for complex agent-resource hierarchies.
    *   **NeMo Guardrails**: NVIDIA's framework for dialog management and safety rails.
    *   **Guardrails AI**: Pydantic-based output validation and safety verification.
*   **Security & Static Analysis**:
    *   `ggshield` (GitGuardian): Integrated via `security-guard.sh` hooks across all 50+ repos for secret prevention.
    *   `semgrep`: Static analysis for security and custom rule enforcement.
    *   `ruff`, `basedpyright`, `tach`: Strict linting, type safety, and code boundary enforcement.
*   **High-Performance Runtimes**:
    *   `granian` / `watchfiles`: Rust-based async HTTP and file watching.
    *   `extism`: WASM runtime for sandboxed plugin execution.

### 2. Research Paper Influence on Logic
External research papers are not just cited but actively guide the implementation of the core "guardian" logic:

*   **Audit & Provenance**: `MAIF (arXiv:2511.15097)` and `Constant-Size Cryptographic Evidence Structures (arXiv:2511.17118)` guide the hash-chained, immutable audit trails.
*   **Safety Rails**: `NeMo Guardrails (arXiv:2310.10501)` provides the foundation for `Colang 2.0` dialog and execution rails.
*   **Reliability**: `Circuit Breakers for AI: Representation Engineering (arXiv:2406.04313)` informs the failure-class taxonomy and token-budget circuit breakers.
*   **Trust Calibration**: `Metacognitive Sensitivity (PNAS Nexus, 2025)` and `Miscalibrated AI Confidence (arXiv:2402.07632)` drive the `TrustScore` and confidence-calibration algorithms.

### 3. Friction-Risk Balance Evaluation

| Category | High Friction | High Risk | Mitigation in Place |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Policy Engines** | **OPA/Rego & Oso/Polar**: Learning a new DSL (Rego/Polar) is a hurdle for developers. | Low risk; industry standard. | **`policy_lib.py`**: A custom Python-based shim for common git-state and local logic to bypass DSL complexity where possible. |
| **Guardrails** | **NeMo Guardrails**: Colang complexity can slow down flow adjustments. | Low risk; prevents hallucination/toxicity. | **Structured Pydantic**: Favoring simple `Guardrails AI` validators for 80% of tasks, reserving NeMo for complex dialog. |
| **Secret Scanning** | **ggshield**: Prevents commits if secrets found; can block workflows. | High risk; secret leaks are critical. | **`security-guard.sh`**: Centralized wrapper that provides clear instructions and bypass paths for false positives. |
| **Infrastructure** | **extism (WASM)**: Adds a build-step friction for plugins. | Low risk; provides superior isolation. | **Pre-compiled artifacts**: Standardizing on WASM-ABI contracts to hide build complexity from consumers. |

### 4. Recommendations

#### **Additions (Reduce Friction/Risk)**:
*   **LangSmith / Arize Phoenix**: Integrate for deep observability into agentic traces. Currently, debugging "why a policy denied an action" is manual; automated tracing would reduce friction.
*   **Trivy**: Add to `security-guard.sh` for automated dependency vulnerability scanning at the hook level.

#### **Removals / Refactors**:
*   **Subprocess Overuse**: Over 60 files currently use `subprocess.run` to call git or file tools. These should be refactored to use FFI (Foreign Function Interface) calls to the existing internal Rust crates (e.g., `thegent-git`, `thegent-hooks`). This reduces the risk of shell injection and significantly lowers runtime friction (latency).
*   **Polyglot Sprawl**: Some logic in `policy-contract` is duplicated across Go and Python. Recommend consolidating the core evaluator into a shared Rust library with FFI bindings.

### 5. Summary
The Phenotype policy and guardian systems are robust and research-grounded. The "friction" introduced by complex engines like OPA is currently mitigated by internal wrappers (`policy_lib.py`). The primary risk remains in the **subprocess-heavy execution model**,
which is currently being addressed by migrating hot paths to Rust-based shims.

Based on the `security-guard-hardening-report.md`, `security-guard-coverage-matrix.csv`, and the supporting documentation in the `policy-contract/` directory, here is a summary of the key points regarding permission contracts and the "Guardian" (Security Guard) feature:

### 1. Permission Contracts
The system uses a **standardized policy scope model** to manage permissions across different environments and agents. These contracts are structured as a hierarchical "stack" that resolves into an effective policy:
*   **Scopes:** `system` (global constraints), `user` (operator), `repo` (repository baseline), `harness` (specific agent like Codex/Cursor), `task_domain` (safety checks), and `task_instance` (one-off overrides).
*   **Resolution:** A Python-based resolver (`resolve.py`) merges these scopes into a single `effective-policy.json` used at runtime.
*   **Rules:** Contracts define `command_rules` with actions like `allow`, `deny`, or `request`.

### 2. Friction vs. Risk Balance
The system balances safety and developer velocity through a **tiered decision model**:
*   **The `request` Action:** Instead of a hard `deny`, the system can trigger a `request`, which prompts the user for manual confirmation. This allows higher-risk commands to proceed under supervision rather than being blocked entirely.
*   **Conditional Authorization:** Rules can be gated by conditions (e.g., `git_clean_worktree`, `git_synced_to_upstream`). If conditions aren't met, the policy can fall back from `allow` to `request` or `deny`, allowing for "safe-by-default" behavior without total lockdown.
*   **Fallbacks:** Manifests include explicit fallback settings (e.g., `missing_policy_default`, `condition_eval_error`) that can be tuned to be "fail-open" (`allow`) or "fail-closed" (`request`/`deny`) based on the environment's risk profile.

### 3. Guardian (Security Guard) & Agent-Based Review
The **Security Guard** is the implementation of the "guardian" concept, deployed across 100% (50/50) of the canonical repositories.
*   **Unified Pipeline:** It provides a unified authorization pipeline that handles both **static command gates** (built into host tools like Cursor and Claude) and **conditional policy checks** (run via external wrappers).
*   **Host Hooks:** A hook (`security-guard.sh`) and an audit workflow ensure that all agent-initiated commands are validated against the resolved policy before execution.
*   **Agent-Based Review:** The system includes "stacked-PR governance" (`lane4`), which acts as an automated reviewer gating workflow transitions by scanning PR status, comments, and security tokens.

### 4. 3rd Party Libraries and Tools
The security architecture integrates several 3rd party harnesses and technical stacks:
*   **Agent Harnesses:** Cursor, Claude, Codex, and Factory-Droid.
*   **Policy & Validation:** Python-based tooling using `uv`, `pyyaml`, `jsonschema`, and `pytest`.
*   **Multi-Language Wrappers:** High-performance policy evaluators implemented in **Go**, **Rust**, and **Zig** to ensure consistent enforcement across different runtime environments.
*   **CI/
CD:** Deep integration with GitHub Actions for automated hardening audits and policy sync.

The changes have been implemented to ensure that users are notified when the guardian (headless review) explicitly allows a command.

### Changes Made

#### `cli/src/policy_federation/claude_hooks.py`
- Modified the `allow` decision path to check for the presence of a `headless_review` result.
- When a command is allowed via guardian arbitration:
    - **Notification Enabled:** Set `suppressOutput` to `False` so the hook message is displayed.
    - **Detailed Reasoning:** Provided a concise `hookSpecificOutput` string that includes the command, the rule that triggered the review, and the guardian's specific reasoning.
- Maintained silence (`suppressOutput: True`) for commands that are allowed directly by policy defaults or explicit rules without needing guardian intervention, preventing unnecessary noise during routine operations.

#### `tests/unit/test_claude_hooks.py`
- Added `test_claude_pretool_hook_notifies_on_guardian_allow` to verify that guardian arbitration results in a visible notification with the expected rule and reasoning details.

I've explored the `policy-contract` repository and identified the structure, existing issues, and how permission requests are handled. Here is the report on the current state and what needs fixing.

### 1. Structure of Permission Contracts
The repository uses a JSON Schema-defined contract (`policy_contract.schema.json`) that governs agent permissions across several layered scopes: `system`, `user`, `repo`, `harness`, `task_domain`, and `task_instance`. 

- **Key Components**:
    - **`commands`**: High-level lists for `allow`, `deny`, and `require`.
    - **`command_rules`**: Fine-grained rules with `action` (`allow`, `request`, `deny`), `match` (exact, glob, prefix), and complex logical `conditions`.
    - **Resolution Logic**: `resolve.py` merges these layers according to a strict precedence order, with `deny` taking the highest priority, followed by `request`, then `allow`.
    - **Implementation**: In `policy_lib.py`, the `require` command is implemented as an alias for the `request` action.

### 2. Broken Permission Request Formats & Inconsistencies
The most significant "broken" or inconsistent format lies in how the `request` action is translated for different agent platforms in `scripts/sync_host_rules.py`:

- **Cursor Friction**: For **Cursor**, the `request` action is mapped directly to `deny`. This means any command that should prompt for permission is instead strictly forbidden, creating high friction for the user.
- **Agent Discrepancy**: 
    - **Claude** maps `request` to `ask` (low friction).
    - **Codex** maps `request` to `prompt` (low friction).
    - **Droid** maps `request` to `commandRequestlist` (low friction).
- **Validation**: Test fixtures like `invalid_bad_action.json` confirm that unsupported actions (e.g., `"maybe"`) are correctly rejected by the validator.

### 3. "Pasted Permission Requests" and Friction-Risk Balance
While "pasted" is not a literal keyword in the code, it refers to the manual approval mechanism handled via "approval tokens."

- **Approval Tokens**: The script `lane4_policy_contract_pr_stack_audit.py` scans GitHub PR titles, bodies, and **comments** for pasted markers like `@stack-approve`, `/policy-stack-approve`, or `approval-token`. This allows human-in-the-loop authorization for sensitive operations.
- **Friction-Risk Balance**:
    - **Friction**: The hard-denial in Cursor for `request` actions is the primary point of friction.
    - **Risk**: The system mitigates risk by defaulting to `request` (manual prompt) in case of policy wrapper failures or missing policies, as seen in `wrappers/policy-wrapper-dispatch.sh`.

### 4. Third-Party Libraries and Tools
The repository relies on standard robust tooling:
- **Python Libraries**: `yaml` (PyYAML), `json`, `argparse`, `shlex`, `fnmatch`.
- **CLI Tools**: `gh` (GitHub CLI) is extensively used for PR auditing and metadata retrieval.
- **Evaluation Wrappers**: Fast evaluation binaries written in **Go**, **Rust**, and **Zig** are used to provide cross-platform policy enforcement.

### What Needs to be Fixed
1.  **Cursor Sync Update**: Update `scripts/sync_host_rules.py` to prevent mapping `request` to `deny` for Cursor. It should ideally provide a path for user confirmation if the platform supports it, or at least distinguish it from a hard `forbidden`.
2.  **Consistently Alias `require`**: Ensure the documentation and schema explicitly reflect that `require` is a functional alias for `request`.
3.  **Parity in Wrappers**: Verify that the Go, Rust, and Zig wrappers all handle the `on_mismatch` fallback consistently, as discrepancies
in fallback behavior can lead to accidental "allow" or "deny" states.

I have improved the logic for "pasted permission requests" in `policy-contract/scripts/lane4_policy_contract_pr_stack_audit.py` to better balance friction and risk.

### Key Improvements:

1.  **Expanded Approval Markers**: Added more natural and flexible markers to the default detection patterns. This reduces the friction for developers who might use simpler terms like `stack-ok`, `stack-ready`, or `approve-stack` instead of strict slash commands.
2.  **Low-Risk Exemption (Friction-Risk Balance)**: 
    *   Implemented a new `_is_low_risk_pr` function that uses `gh pr diff` to check if a PR only contains non-functional changes (e.g., documentation in `*.md`, the `docs/` folder, or `LICENSE` files).
    *   These PRs are now automatically considered exempt from the strict stack-approval requirement, allowing low-risk changes to flow through without manual intervention.
3.  **Bot-Comment Exclusion**: Fixed a potential false-positive bug where the script's own warning comments (which contain the words "approval-token") could be misinterpreted as valid approval signals. The script now explicitly ignores comments from bots and those containing its own signature.
4.  **Enhanced Developer Feedback**: Updated the comment template to be more helpful and less robotic. It now provides clear instructions on how to provide an approval token and mentions the documentation exemption to reduce confusion.

### Changes implemented in:
`/Users/kooshapari/CodeProjects/Phenotype/repos/policy-contract/scripts/lane4_policy_contract_pr_stack_audit.py`

```python
# Expanded patterns
DEFAULT_APPROVAL_TOKEN_PATTERNS = (
    r"/stack-approve\b",
    r"@stack-approve\b",
    r"/stack-merge\b",
    r"/policy-stack-approve\b",
    r"approval token",
    r"approval-token",
    r"approval_token",
    r"stack-ok\b",
    r"stack-ready\b",
    r"approve-stack\b",
    r"merge-stack\b",
    r"stack-approve\b",
)
```

These updates streamline the approval flow for common scenarios while maintaining the
necessary safety checks for functional code changes within PR stacks.

I have modified `scripts/sync_host_rules.py` in the `policy-contract` repository to reduce friction for Cursor rules. 

### Changes made:
1.  **Mapped `request` to `ask` for Cursor**: Rules with the `request` action are now added to a new `ask` array for Cursor, rather than being added to the `deny` array. This ensures that these commands will trigger a prompt instead of being blocked.
2.  **Updated Cursor Rule Generation**: Modified `render_platform_payload` to generate the `ask` array for the Cursor platform, alongside `allow` and `deny`.
3.  **Enhanced Artifact Application**: Updated `_apply_cursor_rules` to read, replace, and write the `ask` array in `cursor.cli-config.json` while preserving existing entries.
4.  **Updated Internal Metrics**: Adjusted `_count_platform_rules`, `_managed_segment_length_after`, and `_had_managed_segment_before` to account for the new `ask` category when calculating rule counts and segment lengths.

These changes ensure that `request` rules for Cursor provide a better user experience by prompting for confirmation rather than failing with a hard denial.
